statement.markdown: Given a partially filled-in Sudoku grid, determine if it is possible to fill in the missing values to make it a valid Sudoku solution.
statement.inputFormat: int[][] board, where each inner array is a row in the grid.
statement.outputFormat: return true if the grid is valid Sudoku, false otherwise.
statement.notes: A valid Sudoku is a 9x9 grid with numbers from 1 to 9, each appearing only once in each row, column and 3x3 sub-grid. The grid is partially filled-in, meaning some cells contain numbers, but others are blank.

hints.text: Check for valid Sudoku by ensuring each row, column and 3x3 sub-grid has numbers from 1 to 9 without duplicates.

editorial.title: Easy Sudoku Backtracking Bug Fix
editorial.intuition: A valid Sudoku can be determined by checking each row, column and 3x3 sub-grid for numbers 1 to 9 without duplicates. We should also use backtracking to fill in missing values.
editorial.bruteForce: Naive solution would be to try all possible numbers for each empty cell and see if it leads to a valid Sudoku.
editorial.optimalSolution: Use backtracking to fill in missing values, and check for valid Sudoku after each move.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: By using backtracking to fill in missing values and checking for valid Sudoku after each move, we ensure a valid Sudoku solution.
editorial.dryRun: Step-by-step example run: Try filling in the missing values in the grid, and check if each row, column and 3x3 sub-grid has numbers from 1 to 9 without duplicates.
commonMistakes: Not checking for valid Sudoku after each move
Not using backtracking to fill in missing values

buggyStarterCode.cpp: class Solution { public: bool isValidSudoku(vector<vector<int>>& board) { if (board[0].size() != 9) return false; for (int i = 0; i < 9; ++i) { for (int j = 0; j < 9; ++j) { if (board[i][j] == 0) continue; int count = 0; for (int k = 0; k < 9; ++k) { if (board[i][k] == board[i][j]) ++count; } if (count != 1) return false; } } return true; } };
buggyStarterCode.java: public class Solution { public boolean isValidSudoku(int[][] board) { if (board[0].length != 9) return false; for (int i = 0; i < 9; ++i) { for (int j = 0; j < 9; ++j) { if (board[i][j] == 0) continue; int count = 0; for (int k = 0; k < 9; ++k) { if (board[i][k] == board[i][j]) ++count; } if (count != 1) return false; } } return true; } }
buggyStarterCode.python: class Solution: def isValidSudoku(self, board): if len(board[0]) != 9: return false for i in range(9): for j in range(9): if board[i][j] == 0: continue count = 0 for k in range(9): if board[i][k] == board[i][j]: count += 1 if count != 1: return false return true
buggyStarterCode.javascript: function isValidSudoku(board) { if (board[0].length != 9) return false; for (let i = 0; i < 9; ++i) { for (let j = 0; j < 9; ++j) { if (board[i][j] == 0) continue; let count = 0; for (let k = 0; k < 9; ++k) { if (board[i][k] == board[i][j]) ++count; } if (count != 1) return false; } } return true; }

solutions.python:
class Solution: def isValidSudoku(self, board: list[list[int]]) -> bool: for i in range(9): for j in range(9): if board[i][j] == 0: continue if board[i][j] < 1 or board[i][j] > 9: return False for row in board: if len(row) != 9: return False if not self.isValidRow(board[i]): return False if not self.isValidCol(board, i): return False if not self.isValidSubBox(board, i, j): return False return True 

def isValidRow(board, row): seen = set() for i in range(9): if board[row][i] == 0: continue if board[row][i] in seen: return False seen.add(board[row][i]) return True 

def isValidCol(board, col, row): seen = set() for i in range(9): if board[i][col] == 0: continue if board[i][col] in seen: return False seen.add(board[i][col]) return True 

def isValidSubBox(board, row, col): seen = set() for i in range(3): for j in range(3): r = row + i; c = col + j if board[r][c] == 0: continue if board[r][c] in seen: return False seen.add(board[r][c]) return True
